---
title: "Mesfin Genie"
---

<div class="hero">
  <div class="hero-inner">
    <div>
      <div class="eyebrow">Health Economics · Choice Modelling · Behavioural Science</div>
      <h1>Mesfin Genie, PhD</h1>
      <h2>Senior Lecturer in Health Economics<br>Newcastle Business School · The University of Newcastle</h2>
      <p>I study how patients, citizens and decision makers make trade-offs in complex health and policy settings. My work combines health economics, discrete choice experiments, behavioural economics, eye tracking, preference measurement, and applied policy evaluation.</p>
      <div class="hero-buttons">
        <a class="btn-site btn-primary" href="publications.html">Publications</a>
        <a class="btn-site btn-secondary" href="research.html">Research areas</a>
        <a class="btn-site btn-secondary" href="cv.html">CV</a>
        <a class="btn-site btn-secondary" href="contact.html">Contact</a>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="profile-card">
      <div class="portrait-placeholder">MG</div>
      <div class="profile-meta"><strong>Research focus</strong><br>Health preference research, discrete choice experiments, information processing, vaccine policy, health technology assessment, and decision support tools.</div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<section class="section">

## Research at a glance

<div class="grid grid-3">
<div class="card"><div class="metric">Health</div><div class="metric-label">Preference research and policy evaluation</div></div>
<div class="card"><div class="metric">DCE</div><div class="metric-label">Discrete choice experiments and choice modelling</div></div>
<div class="card"><div class="metric">Behaviour</div><div class="metric-label">Attention, information processing and decision quality</div></div>
</div>

</section>

<section class="section">

## Core research fields

<div class="grid grid-3">
<div class="card"><h3>Health economics and policy</h3><p>Economic evaluation, health system reform, public health policy, health technology assessment and policy acceptability.</p></div>
<div class="card"><h3>Choice modelling</h3><p>Discrete choice experiments, mixed logit, latent class models, heterogeneity, equity–efficiency trade-offs and stated preference methods.</p></div>
<div class="card"><h3>Behavioural health economics</h3><p>Attention, numeracy, feedback, attribute processing, eye tracking and decision quality in health preference tasks.</p></div>
</div>

</section>

<section class="section">

## Selected recent work

<div class="pub"><div class="pub-field">Health Economics</div><div class="pub-title">Guidance or Misdirection? Unpacking the Role of Feedback in Health Preference Assessments</div><div class="pub-meta">Genie, Reed and Ozdemir · 2026 · Health Economics</div></div>
<div class="pub"><div class="pub-field">Health Policy</div><div class="pub-title">Feeling lonely? Preferences for support programmes to reduce loneliness among older adults in Australia</div><div class="pub-meta">Genie, Boyes and Paolucci · 2026 · Health Policy</div></div>
<div class="pub"><div class="pub-field">Health Economics</div><div class="pub-title">Keeping an eye on cost: What can eye tracking tell us about attention to cost information in discrete choice experiments?</div><div class="pub-meta">Genie · 2023 · Health Economics</div></div>

</section>

<section class="section">

## Current priorities

<div class="grid grid-2">
<div class="card"><h3>Vaccine mandate evaluation</h3><p>Mandate design, public acceptability, international preference heterogeneity and decision tools for future pandemic policy.</p></div>
<div class="card"><h3>Attention and choice</h3><p>Using eye tracking and experimental manipulations to understand how information presentation changes preferences and choice quality.</p></div>
<div class="card"><h3>Decision support tools</h3><p>Online tools translating DCE and economic evaluation evidence into transparent policy and clinical decision support.</p></div>
<div class="card"><h3>Equity and value assessment</h3><p>Incorporating concern for worse-off groups, distributional preferences and fairness into preference-based value assessment.</p></div>
</div>

</section>
